sectionName: Biological and Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
A genetic counselor analyzes a family with a rare metabolic disorder that causes accumulation of a toxic substrate in affected tissues. The disorder is suspected to follow autosomal recessive inheritance. The pedigree (Figure 1) shows three generations: unaffected parents (I-1 and I-2) have three children — an affected son (II-1), an unaffected daughter (II-2) who marries an unaffected male (II-3), and an unaffected son (II-4). Generation III includes one unaffected son (III-1) and one affected daughter (III-2), born to II-2 and II-3.

The counselor notes that the disease allele frequency in the general population is 1/50 (q = 0.02). Carrier testing reveals that II-2 is heterozygous (Aa). The counselor uses Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (p² + 2pq + q² = 1) and Mendelian probability calculations to assess carrier risks for unaffected family members.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Pedigree showing three generations of a family with a rare metabolic disorder. Squares represent males; circles represent females. Filled symbols indicate affected individuals.
questions.stem: Based on the pedigree, what are the genotypes of individuals I-1 and I-2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both are homozygous dominant (AA)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both are heterozygous carriers (Aa)

choices.label: C
choices.text: I-1 is Aa and I-2 is AA

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both are homozygous recessive (aa)

explanations.A: If both parents were AA, they could not produce an affected (aa) child. II-1 is affected, meaning he must have received one recessive allele from each parent. Both parents must carry at least one copy of the disease allele.
explanations.B: Correct. II-1 is affected (aa), meaning he inherited one 'a' allele from each parent. Since both I-1 and I-2 are unaffected, they cannot be aa. Therefore both must be heterozygous carriers (Aa). This is the classic autosomal recessive pattern: two unaffected carrier parents producing affected offspring with a 1/4 probability per pregnancy. The pedigree confirms this — unaffected parents with an affected child is the hallmark of autosomal recessive inheritance.
explanations.C: If I-2 were AA, she could only pass the 'A' allele to offspring. II-1 would then have genotype Aa at most, which would be unaffected (since the disorder is recessive). Since II-1 is affected (aa), both parents must contribute an 'a' allele, requiring both to be at least Aa.
explanations.D: If both parents were aa, they would both be affected. The pedigree shows I-1 and I-2 as unaffected (open symbols), ruling out the aa genotype for either parent.

questions.stem: What is the probability that individual II-4 is a carrier (Aa)?

explanations.A: 1/4 is the probability of being homozygous recessive (aa) from an Aa × Aa cross. Among all offspring, 1/4 are AA, 2/4 are Aa, and 1/4 are aa. But since II-4 is unaffected, we must exclude the aa possibility and recalculate among the remaining genotypes.
explanations.B: 1/2 is the probability of being Aa among all offspring of an Aa × Aa cross (before knowing phenotype). However, since II-4 is unaffected, we know he is not aa. Using conditional probability: P(Aa | unaffected) = P(Aa)/P(unaffected) = (1/2)/(3/4) = 2/3.
explanations.C: Correct. From Aa × Aa: 1/4 AA, 2/4 Aa, 1/4 aa. Since II-4 is unaffected, he is either AA (1/4) or Aa (2/4). Among the 3/4 who are unaffected, 2/4 are carriers. P(carrier | unaffected) = (2/4)/(3/4) = 2/3. This is a critical probability for genetic counseling — any unaffected sibling of an affected individual from carrier parents has a 2/3 chance of being a carrier.
explanations.D: 3/4 is the probability of being unaffected (either AA or Aa), not the conditional probability of being a carrier given unaffected status. The question asks specifically about carrier status, which requires the Bayesian calculation excluding the aa genotype from the denominator.

questions.stem: II-2 is confirmed as a carrier (Aa). If II-3 is from the general population (disease allele frequency q = 0.02), what is the probability that II-3 is a carrier?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2pq ≈ 0.04 or about 1/25

explanations.A: Correct. Using Hardy-Weinberg: carrier frequency = 2pq, where q = 0.02 and p = 1 - q = 0.98. So 2pq = 2(0.98)(0.02) = 0.0392 ≈ 0.04, or approximately 1 in 25. Since II-3 has no family history of the disorder (he married into the family), his carrier probability is determined by the population carrier frequency. This is substantially lower than the 2/3 probability for family members like II-4.
explanations.B: q² = (0.02)² = 0.0004 represents the frequency of affected individuals (aa) in the population, not carriers (Aa). The carrier frequency (2pq) is much higher than the affected frequency because most disease alleles exist in heterozygous carriers rather than in affected homozygotes.
explanations.C: 2/3 is the probability for an unaffected sibling of an affected individual from known carrier parents (as calculated for II-4). II-3 has no family connection to the disease — he married into the family. His carrier probability is determined by population genetics, not family pedigree analysis.
explanations.D: 1/2 is the carrier probability for offspring of one known carrier parent crossed with a non-carrier. Without additional information, II-3's carrier status is estimated from population frequencies, not family-specific probabilities.

questions.stem: Given that II-2 is Aa and II-3 has a carrier probability of ~1/25, what is the probability that their next child will be affected?

explanations.A: 1/4 would be correct only if both parents were confirmed carriers (Aa × Aa). However, II-3's carrier status is uncertain — his probability of being Aa is only 1/25. The overall risk must account for this uncertainty by multiplying the probability that II-3 is a carrier by the conditional probability of an affected child given that he is.
explanations.B: Correct. P(affected child) = P(II-3 is Aa) × P(affected | Aa × Aa) = (1/25) × (1/4) = 1/100. There is also a small probability that II-3 is aa (affected, which is ruled out since he is unaffected) or AA (in which case no child can be affected). The calculation accounts for the uncertainty in II-3's genotype. In genetic counseling, this risk (1%) is communicated to the couple as the prior probability before any testing.
explanations.C: 1/200 would result from using a carrier frequency of 1/50 (which is the disease allele frequency q, not the carrier frequency 2pq ≈ 1/25). The correct carrier frequency from Hardy-Weinberg is 2pq ≈ 1/25, giving 1/25 × 1/4 = 1/100.
explanations.D: 1/50 is the disease allele frequency (q), not the probability of an affected child. The probability of an affected child requires multiplying the probability that II-3 is a carrier (1/25) by the Mendelian probability of an affected child from a carrier cross (1/4).

questions.stem: However, III-2 is affected (aa). Given this new information, what is the updated probability that II-3 is a carrier?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 0% — II-3 cannot be a carrier if III-2 is affected

choices.label: B
choices.text: ~1/25 — the prior probability is unchanged by offspring phenotypes

choices.label: C
choices.text: 100% — II-3 must be a carrier because he has an affected daughter

choices.label: D
choices.text: 50% — the probability increases but cannot reach certainty

explanations.A: For III-2 to be affected (aa), she must inherit an 'a' allele from each parent. Since II-2 is confirmed Aa, she can provide one 'a.' The second 'a' must come from II-3, proving he carries at least one 'a' allele. He is unaffected, so he must be Aa, not aa.
explanations.B: Bayesian updating requires incorporating new evidence. The birth of an affected child is strong evidence about parental genotype. The prior probability of 1/25 must be updated given that III-2 is aa, which can only occur if II-3 contributed an 'a' allele.
explanations.C: Correct. III-2 is aa, meaning she inherited one 'a' from II-2 (confirmed Aa) and one 'a' from II-3. Since II-3 is unaffected (not aa), he must be Aa. The posterior probability of II-3 being a carrier, given that he has an affected child with a known carrier, is 100%. This is an example of Bayesian updating — the birth of an affected child conclusively establishes the father's carrier status, overriding the prior population-based estimate.
explanations.D: There is no uncertainty remaining. For an autosomal recessive trait, an affected child must receive one recessive allele from each parent. Since II-2 is Aa and III-2 is aa, II-3 must have contributed the second 'a' allele. He cannot be AA (that would make III-2 either Aa or AA, both unaffected). The probability is exactly 100%, not 50%.

questions.passage:
A molecular genetics lab identifies the gene responsible for the metabolic disorder. The gene encodes a lysosomal enzyme, and the disease-causing mutation is a single nucleotide substitution (c.1234G>A) that creates a premature stop codon (p.Trp412Ter). The lab develops a PCR-based diagnostic test using restriction fragment length polymorphism (RFLP) analysis. The wild-type allele contains a recognition site for the restriction enzyme BamHI at the mutation position; the mutant allele lacks this site due to the G>A substitution.

Wild-type DNA digested with BamHI produces two fragments (200 bp and 300 bp). Mutant DNA produces one uncut fragment (500 bp). The lab runs gel electrophoresis on samples from all family members.
questions.stem: On the RFLP gel, a carrier individual (Aa) would show which banding pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One band at 500 bp only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two bands at 200 bp and 300 bp only

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three bands at 200 bp, 300 bp, and 500 bp

choices.label: D
choices.text: Two bands at 250 bp each

explanations.A: One band at 500 bp would indicate homozygous mutant (aa): both alleles lack the BamHI site, so no cutting occurs and only the intact 500 bp fragment appears. A carrier has one wild-type allele that is cut by BamHI.
explanations.B: Two bands at 200 and 300 bp would indicate homozygous wild-type (AA): both alleles contain the BamHI site and are cleaved into 200 and 300 bp fragments. A carrier also has one uncut mutant allele producing a 500 bp band.
explanations.C: Correct. A carrier (Aa) has one wild-type allele (with the BamHI site) and one mutant allele (lacking the site). The wild-type allele is cut into 200 bp and 300 bp fragments. The mutant allele remains uncut at 500 bp. The gel therefore shows three bands: 200, 300, and 500 bp. This three-band pattern is diagnostic for heterozygous carrier status and is the hallmark of RFLP analysis for detecting carrier status in autosomal recessive disorders.
explanations.D: BamHI cuts at the specific recognition sequence, producing fragments of predetermined sizes (200 and 300 bp). Equal-sized fragments (250 bp each) would require the cut to occur exactly at the midpoint of the 500 bp fragment, which depends on where the restriction site is located. The passage specifies asymmetric cutting into 200 and 300 bp fragments.

questions.stem: The disease-causing mutation creates a premature stop codon. The resulting protein would most likely be:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a full-length protein with a single amino acid substitution that may retain partial function

choices.label: B
choices.text: a truncated, nonfunctional protein due to early termination of translation

choices.label: C
choices.text: a protein with enhanced enzymatic activity due to the conformational change

choices.label: D
choices.text: a protein of normal length but altered post-translational modification

explanations.A: A missense mutation produces a single amino acid substitution and may retain partial function. However, the passage specifies a nonsense mutation (p.Trp412Ter) — a premature stop codon replaces tryptophan at position 412. Translation terminates at this premature stop, producing a truncated protein, not a full-length one with a substitution.
explanations.B: Correct. The mutation p.Trp412Ter converts a tryptophan codon (UGG) to a stop codon (UGA, UAG, or UAA) at position 412. Translation terminates prematurely, producing a truncated protein missing all amino acids from position 412 onward. This truncated protein almost certainly lacks the catalytic domain or structural elements needed for lysosomal enzyme function. Additionally, mRNAs with premature stop codons are often degraded by nonsense-mediated mRNA decay (NMD), further reducing protein levels. Loss of this lysosomal enzyme leads to substrate accumulation — the hallmark of lysosomal storage diseases.
explanations.C: Premature termination produces a shorter protein missing C-terminal residues. A truncated enzyme that lacks structural or catalytic domains cannot have enhanced activity. Gain-of-function mutations exist but involve specific amino acid changes that alter regulation or substrate specificity, not premature chain termination.
explanations.D: A premature stop codon produces a shorter protein, not one of normal length with altered modification. Post-translational modifications (glycosylation, phosphorylation) occur on existing amino acid residues; if those residues are missing due to truncation, modifications cannot occur. Even if the truncated protein is modified, it would still be shorter and likely nonfunctional.

questions.stem: The enzyme deficiency causes accumulation of a substrate in lysosomes. This type of disease is classified as a:

choices.label: A
choices.text: chromosomal aneuploidy syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: mitochondrial myopathy

choices.label: C
choices.text: trinucleotide repeat disorder

choices.label: D
choices.text: lysosomal storage disease

explanations.A: Aneuploidy (abnormal chromosome number) results from nondisjunction during meiosis, producing conditions like trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) or monosomy X (Turner syndrome). The described disorder results from a single gene mutation following Mendelian inheritance, not a chromosomal number abnormality.
explanations.B: Mitochondrial myopathies result from mutations in mitochondrial DNA or nuclear genes encoding mitochondrial proteins, causing impaired oxidative phosphorylation. They primarily affect muscle and nerve tissue and follow maternal inheritance (if mtDNA-encoded) or Mendelian patterns (if nuclear-encoded). The described condition involves lysosomal enzyme deficiency, not mitochondrial dysfunction.
explanations.C: Trinucleotide repeat disorders (Huntington disease, fragile X syndrome, myotonic dystrophy) result from expansion of repeating three-nucleotide sequences in specific genes. The described mutation is a single nucleotide substitution creating a premature stop codon, not a repeat expansion.
explanations.D: Correct. Lysosomal storage diseases result from deficiency of lysosomal enzymes needed to degrade specific substrates. Without the enzyme, the substrate accumulates within lysosomes, causing cellular dysfunction and organ damage. Classic examples include Tay-Sachs disease (hexosaminidase A deficiency, GM2 ganglioside accumulation), Gaucher disease (glucocerebrosidase deficiency), and Fabry disease (α-galactosidase A deficiency). Most follow autosomal recessive inheritance, consistent with this family's pedigree.

questions.stem: If II-4 (probability 2/3 of being Aa) marries a woman from the general population, what is the probability their first child will be affected?

explanations.A: Correct. P(affected) = P(II-4 is Aa) × P(wife is Aa) × P(aa child | Aa×Aa) = (2/3)(1/25)(1/4) = 2/300 = 1/150. This compound probability accounts for uncertainty in both parents' genotypes. The relatively low risk (0.67%) reflects the low population carrier frequency, making it unlikely that both parents carry the disease allele.
explanations.B: 2/75 does not correspond to any valid calculation using the given probabilities. The correct answer requires: P(II-4 is Aa) × P(wife is Aa) × P(aa child | both Aa) = (2/3)(1/25)(1/4).
explanations.C: 1/300 would result from using 1/3 instead of 2/3 for II-4's carrier probability, or from a factor-of-2 error in the population carrier frequency calculation. The correct carrier probability for an unaffected sibling of an affected individual from Aa × Aa parents is 2/3, giving (2/3)(1/25)(1/4) = 1/150.
explanations.D: 1/75 would result from omitting the 1/4 Mendelian probability: (2/3)(1/25) = 2/75 ≈ 1/37.5. The full calculation must include the probability of the aa genotype from an Aa × Aa cross.

questions.stem: Genetic testing reveals that III-1 is a carrier (Aa). If III-1 marries a carrier woman (Aa), what is the probability that their first two children are both unaffected?

explanations.A: 1/16 is the probability that both children are affected: (1/4)² = 1/16. The question asks about both being unaffected, which has the complementary probability structure: (3/4)² = 9/16.
explanations.B: 3/4 is the probability that a single child from Aa × Aa is unaffected. For two children both to be unaffected, the probabilities must be multiplied: (3/4)(3/4) = 9/16. Each child is an independent event.
explanations.C: 1/2 is the probability of being a carrier (Aa) from an Aa × Aa cross, not the probability of being unaffected. The probability of being unaffected includes both AA (1/4) and Aa (2/4) genotypes, totaling 3/4 per child.
explanations.D: Correct. From Aa × Aa: P(unaffected) = 3/4 per child (1/4 AA + 2/4 Aa). For two independent children both unaffected: (3/4)² = 9/16. Each pregnancy is an independent event, so probabilities multiply. 9/16 = 56.25%, meaning there is a substantial (~44%) chance that at least one of two children will be affected. This calculation is fundamental to genetic counseling for family planning.
